project.name: Quill
project.description: Post-quantum encrypted P2P messenger. Classical asymmetric algorithms (RSA, ECDH) are vulnerable to Shor's algorithm on quantum computers. This project implements a fully quantum-safe communication protocol based on NIST PQC standards (FIPS 203, 204, 205) published in 2024.
project.future_use: Base for engineering thesis on intelligent PQC algorithm selection

priorities.primary: SECURITY — every decision must prioritize cryptographic correctness over convenience
priorities.secondary: CORRECTNESS — working crypto before any UI or optimization
priorities.tertiary: CLARITY — code must be readable and defensible in academic review
anti_goals: Do NOT reimplement crypto primitives — use liboqs and OpenSSL
Do NOT reuse nonces under any circumstance
Do NOT skip signature verification steps
Do NOT use classical asymmetric crypto (RSA, ECDH, DH) anywhere

done: liboqs built and installed from source
crypto.hpp — AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt with random nonce
main.cpp — working CLI demo: server + client, Kyber handshake over TCP, encrypted chat
not_done: Full handshake protocol with signature verification

stack.crypto_pqc: liboqs (Open Quantum Safe) — MIT license, built from source
stack.frontend: undecided — CLI for now, possibly ImGui or React+WebSocket later

MAX.notes: hash-based, 10-950x slower than FALCON, most secure

session_encryption.rationale: quantum-safe — Grover reduces to 128-bit effective security, still secure
session_encryption.key_derivation: first 32 bytes of Kyber shared_secret (demo), HKDF in production
session_encryption.nonce: 12 bytes, random per message via RAND_bytes — NEVER reuse
session_encryption.tag: 16 bytes GCM auth tag appended to ciphertext

forward_secrecy.description: New Kyber keypair generated per session

critical_rules: Never reuse a nonce with the same key
Every message chunk gets its own nonce
Always verify Dilithium signatures before trusting any key material
Never fall back to classical crypto

protocol.transport: TCP with length-prefixed framing: uint32_t (big-endian) + payload bytes

steps.name: SessionEstablished
steps.result: AES-256 key = KDF(shared_secret), all further data encrypted with AES-256-GCM

schema.type: string — message | file_chunk | file_end | handshake | ping
schema.file_hash_sha3: string — base64, SHA-3 hash of full file

architecture.concurrency: std::thread per client, std::mutex for shared state

files.network.hpp: TCP server/client, send_msg/recv_msg with length prefix — DONE
files.main.cpp: CLI entry point, server/client modes — DONE (basic)

known_issues.pqc_key_sizes: Kyber-1024 public key ~1568B vs 32B in ECDH — protocol must handle large messages
known_issues.nonce_reuse: CRITICAL — AES-GCM is catastrophically broken if nonce reused with same key
known_issues.falcon_fpu: FALCON-512 requires FPU for efficient operation
known_issues.sphincs_speed: SPHINCS+ is 10-950x slower than FALCON for signing — only use in MAX mode
known_issues.nat_traversal: P2P across different networks requires NAT traversal — not yet addressed
known_issues.kdf: Current demo uses raw shared_secret bytes as AES key — should use HKDF in production

next_steps_priority_order: 5. Handshake visualization with per-step timing in ms
7. File transfer with 64KB chunking + SHA-3 verification
8. Google Test unit tests for all crypto operations

ai_assistant_instructions.language: Respond in Polish unless asked otherwise
ai_assistant_instructions.focus: Security correctness above all else — always flag any crypto misuse
ai_assistant_instructions.style: Prefer clear, readable code over clever one-liners — this is academic code
ai_assistant_instructions.libs: Use liboqs for all PQC, OpenSSL for AES/SHA/RAND — do not suggest other crypto libs
when_suggesting_code: Always handle OQS_KEM_free and OQS_SIG_free to avoid memory leaks
Always use RAND_bytes for nonce generation, never deterministic values
Always verify signatures before using any received key material
Prefer std::vector<uint8_t> (aliased as Bytes) for raw byte buffers
Use C++23 features freely (structured bindings, ranges, etc.)
